splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on when actuarial assumptions used to calculate withdrawal liability under ERISA must be finalized. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans, as the timing directly controls how much withdrawing employers must pay.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that the plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing use of more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be fixed by the end of the prior plan year (the statutory measurement date), providing employers with greater predictability about potential liability.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: The circuits are split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year and raises fundamental questions about judicial power to extend punishment without explicit statutory authorization.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, pausing the clock and extending the period during which violations can be found.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release is not tolled during absconding because there is no statutory basis for the judicially-created tolling doctrine, and the term expires as specified in the judgment.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: The circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face a categorical bar under § 2244(b)(1) from raising any claim they previously presented in a successive § 2255 motion, in addition to § 2255(h)'s separate requirements. This affects how strictly the gatekeeping requirements apply to federal prisoners seeking successive collateral attacks on their convictions.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, categorically barring any claim previously presented.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s bar does not apply to federal prisoners filing § 2255 motions, meaning they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of showing newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on the standing requirements for candidates to bring pre-election challenges to election rules. The split implicates how easily pre-election challenges to voting procedures can be brought nationwide and raises practical concerns about whether denying pre-election standing would funnel disputes into post-election litigation.

positions.label: Candidates have standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates have Article III standing to challenge election rules pre-election based on competitive injury or vote-dilution theories.

positions.label: Candidates lack standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates lack Article III standing to challenge state election laws before the election because they have not suffered a concrete injury.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes priority over agency or Sentencing Commission deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions.
splits.description: The circuits are split on whether lenity trumps deference to agency interpretations in the criminal context. At least three circuits hold that lenity prevails over deference, while the Third Circuit has held that agency deference trumps lenity. The issue has become more salient after the Supreme Court's decision in Loper Bright overruling Chevron deference.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes priority over agency or Sentencing Commission deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through delegated power.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference takes priority over the rule of lenity, allowing deference to anti-defendant interpretations of ambiguous guidelines or regulations.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion is disqualifying).
splits.description: The circuits are deeply split on whether 'and' means 'and' or 'or' in the safety-valve provision. The Ninth and Eleventh Circuits read the provision conjunctively (all three criteria must be met to disqualify), while the Fifth, Sixth, Seventh, and Eighth Circuits read it disjunctively (any one criterion disqualifies). This directly affects whether defendants can be sentenced below mandatory minimums.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which includes inchoate offenses like conspiracy in the definition of 'controlled substance offense' for the career-offender enhancement, is entitled to deference when the guideline text does not mention inchoate offenses.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether the Sentencing Commission's commentary expanding 'controlled substance offense' to include conspiracy and other inchoate offenses is a valid interpretation entitled to deference under Stinson/Kisor, or exceeds the guideline's unambiguous text. This affects whether prior conspiracy convictions trigger the career-offender enhancement and its significantly higher sentencing range.

positions.label: Commentary is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1's inclusion of inchoate offenses is a reasonable interpretation of an ambiguous guideline and is entitled to controlling weight under Stinson and Kisor.

positions.label: Commentary exceeds guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the definition beyond what the text supports, so deference is inappropriate.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law (such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c)) may be considered as a factor supporting 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: The circuits are split on whether courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as part of the extraordinary-and-compelling-reasons analysis for compassionate release. Some circuits hold that such changes can never contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons, while others permit courts to consider them as one factor. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment to U.S.S.G. § 1B1.13(b)(6) attempted to resolve this issue but some circuits have held the amendment exceeds the Commission's statutory authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law may be considered, alone or in combination with other factors, as contributing to extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot be considered
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, whether offered alone or in combination with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a prior state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state law covers substances not listed on the federal controlled substances schedules.
splits.description: The circuits are split on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires the substance to be federally scheduled. Some circuits hold the definition broadly encompasses any state drug offense regardless of federal scheduling, while others require the substance to be federally controlled. The Supreme Court's decision in Brown v. United States addressed a related but distinct issue.

positions.label: State schedule controls — no federal limitation
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines broadly define 'controlled substance offense' to include any offense under state law, and there is no textual basis to graft a federal scheduling limitation onto the guideline.

positions.label: Federal schedule required
positions.summary: Holds that a prior state conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines only if the substance is also federally scheduled, applying a categorical approach limited to federal law.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners must use habeas corpus (28 U.S.C. § 2241) as their exclusive remedy for challenges to conditions or execution of confinement that affect the duration of custody, or whether civil rights actions (e.g., Bivens) are also available.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether habeas corpus is the exclusive federal remedy for prisoner challenges that affect the duration of confinement but are not pure fact-or-duration-of-confinement claims under Preiser. This affects how federal prisoners can challenge BOP decisions regarding earned time credits, transfers to prerelease custody, and similar administrative determinations under the First Step Act.

positions.label: Habeas is the exclusive remedy
positions.summary: Holds that when a prisoner's challenge relates to the fact or duration of confinement, including BOP decisions on prerelease custody and earned time credits, habeas corpus is the sole federal remedy.

positions.label: Civil rights actions also available
positions.summary: Holds that conditions-of-confinement claims that do not directly challenge the fact or duration of imprisonment may proceed as civil rights actions even when habeas might also be available.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may expressly consider punishment as a purpose of a sentence imposed upon revocation of supervised release, or whether revocation sentences must be limited to sanctioning the breach of trust.
splits.description: The Sixth Circuit permits district courts to expressly punish defendants for the underlying conduct leading to revocation of supervised release, placing it at the extreme end of a circuit split. Other circuits limit the purpose of revocation sentences to sanctioning the breach of the court's trust, not punishing the new criminal conduct.

positions.label: Punishment for underlying conduct permitted
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for the violations of supervised release conditions, including the underlying criminal conduct, when imposing revocation sentences.

positions.label: Revocation sentences limited to sanctioning breach of trust
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sentences should be based on the defendant's breach of the court's trust in violating supervised release conditions, not as punishment for the new underlying criminal conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to an appeal from the denial of a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment in a criminal case.
splits.description: The circuits are split on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees in a criminal case is a civil matter (subject to the 30-day appeal deadline) or a criminal matter (subject to the 14-day deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies because a Hyde Amendment motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, and thus the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion arises from and is part of the criminal case, so the 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) governs.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert in good faith from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: The circuits are split on whether appreciation in property value that occurs between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtors. The Ninth Circuit held that such appreciation belongs to the estate, creating a split with other circuits that hold appreciation belongs to the debtors.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), coupled with the broad scope of § 541(a), means that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtors
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtors under the structure, purpose, and legislative history of § 348(f), which was designed to protect good-faith converting debtors.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether claims that could also be raised in a habeas petition under § 2255 — such as allegations of legal error in the underlying conviction — may be considered as part of the compassionate-release analysis. Some circuits allow consideration of such factors while others prohibit it, viewing compassionate release as an improper substitute for habeas.

positions.label: May consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider factors related to potential errors in a defendant's conviction or sentence as one component of the extraordinary-and-compelling-reasons analysis under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: May not consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release may not be used as a vehicle to challenge the underlying conviction or sentence, and such claims must be raised through habeas corpus under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: The circuits are split on whether FAAAA's broad preemption provision, which prohibits states from enacting or enforcing laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any motor carrier,' extends to state negligence claims against freight brokers for injuries arising from their selection of motor carriers. This affects liability exposure for the freight brokerage industry nationwide.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted by FAAAA because they relate to the broker's service of arranging transportation.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted because they are too tenuously connected to broker services to fall within FAAAA's preemption clause.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) clearly authorizes individual capacity damages suits against state prison officials, and whether Congress has the constitutional power under the Spending Clause to impose such personal liability.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. This split affects the remedies available to prisoners whose religious exercise is substantially burdened and raises significant Spending Clause questions about Congress's power to impose personal liability on individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA's text authorizes suits for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Landor v. Louisiana Dept. of Corrections

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not authorize money damages against prison officials in their individual capacities, limiting relief to injunctive and declaratory remedies against officials in their official capacities.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court order denying a motion to seal documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: The circuits have not uniformly addressed whether orders denying motions to seal medical records qualify for immediate appeal under the collateral order doctrine. The D.C. Circuit held that such orders are immediately appealable because medical privacy is a value of 'high order' that would be effectively unreviewable after final judgment, while other circuits have been more restrictive in applying the collateral order doctrine to sealing orders.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that denial of a motion to seal documents referencing medical treatments and diagnoses is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of high order effectively unreviewable after final judgment.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that denial of a motion to seal is not immediately appealable because the interest in sealing can be adequately vindicated through other means, including appeal after final judgment.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to read the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly for last-mile delivery workers who transport goods within a single state as part of an interstate chain of commerce. This affects whether such workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad reading — last-mile workers exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are the last link in an interstate chain of commerce, even if operating within a single state, qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce and are exempt from the FAA.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Fraga v. Premium Retail Services, Inc.

positions.label: Narrow reading — last-mile workers not exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods locally within a state do not qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce merely because the goods previously moved in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether FIFRA's labeling requirements preempt state tort claims alleging that a pesticide manufacturer should have included additional warnings on its label. This affects products liability litigation involving herbicides and pesticides nationwide, including high-profile Roundup litigation.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims because allowing such claims would impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' those required under FIFRA.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state failure-to-warn claims are not preempted by FIFRA because they parallel, rather than impose requirements 'in addition to or different from,' federal labeling requirements.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of race discrimination under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, particularly regarding the scope of the right to make and enforce contracts?
splits.description: Circuits have taken different approaches to the standard for § 1981 claims in hospitality and service contexts, particularly regarding what constitutes interference with the right to make and enforce contracts and the available remedies. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve the Second Circuit's approach against other circuits' frameworks.

positions.label: Narrower standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 claims require a showing of interference with an actual contractual relationship or attempted contract, applying a more restrictive view of what constitutes actionable discrimination.

positions.label: Broader standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 covers a broader range of discriminatory conduct in business establishments, including degraded service or hostile treatment that impairs the enjoyment of contracted services.
